section.title: The Basket of Ripe Fruit
section.sectionRationale: The fourth vision is a basket of summer fruit, ripe and ready, and God turns it into a pun: the end has ripened for Israel. Amos then returns to the sin that drives the sentence, merchants who cannot wait for the Sabbath to end so they can cheat the poor, and to the terrors of that day: the land quaking, the sun going dark at noon, feasts turned to mourning. At the center stands the most chilling threat of all, a famine not of bread but of hearing the words of the LORD.
section.thesis: Israel is ripe for judgment because a people who trample the needy and cheat the poor while keeping the outward forms of religion have exhausted God's patience; and the severest judgment is not economic or natural disaster but the withdrawal of God's word itself.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the vision of the ripe fruit and the announced end; the greed that cannot wait for the Sabbath to be over; and the day of darkness crowned by a famine of hearing God's words.

verses.text: This is what the Lord GOD showed me: I saw a basket of {{qayits|summer fruit}}.
“Amos, what do you see?” He asked. “A basket of summer fruit,” I replied. So the LORD said to me, “The {{qets|end}} has come for My people Israel; I will no longer spare them.”
“In that day,” declares the Lord GOD, “the songs of the temple will turn to wailing. Many will be the corpses, strewn in silence everywhere!”

units.label: Greed that cannot wait for the Sabbath
units.note: trampling the needy, cheating with scales

verses.text: Hear this, you who trample the needy, who do away with the poor of the land,

asking, “When will the New Moon be over, that we may sell grain? When will the Sabbath end, that we may market wheat? Let us reduce the ephah and increase the shekel; let us cheat with dishonest scales.
Let us buy the poor with silver and the needy for a pair of sandals, selling even the chaff with the wheat!”
The LORD has sworn by the Pride of Jacob: “I will never forget any of their deeds.

units.label: The day of darkness and the famine of the word

verses.text: Will not the land quake for this, and all its dwellers mourn? All of it will swell like the Nile; it will surge and then subside like the Nile in Egypt.
And in that day, declares the Lord GOD, I will make the sun go down at noon, and I will darken the earth in the daytime.

I will turn your feasts into mourning and all your songs into lamentation. I will cause everyone to wear sackcloth and every head to be shaved. I will make it like a time of mourning for an only son, and its outcome like a bitter day.
Behold, the days are coming, declares the Lord GOD, when I will send a {{raab|famine}} on the land, not a famine of bread or a thirst for water, but a famine of hearing the words of the LORD.
People will stagger from sea to sea and roam from north to east, seeking the {{dabar|word}} of the LORD, but they will not find it.
In that day the lovely young women, the young men as well, will faint from thirst.
Those who swear by the guilt of Samaria and say, ‘As surely as your god lives, O Dan,’ or, ‘As surely as the way of Beersheba lives,’ they will fall, never to rise again.”

qayits.gloss: Summer fruit, the late harvest gathered at the end of the agricultural year, ripe and ready to eat but quick to spoil. The basket of qayits looks like a picture of blessing and plenty, yet God turns it into a sign of doom. Ripe summer fruit marks the very end of the season; so Israel, seemingly at the height of prosperity, is in fact ready for the end.

qets.gloss: The end, the terminus. It sounds almost identical to qayits, summer fruit, and God exploits the wordplay: the basket of qayits declares that the qets has come for Israel. The pun turns an image of harvest into a sentence of finality. Just as ripe fruit signals the close of the year, the nation's time has run out; the LORD will no longer spare them.

raab.gloss: Famine, hunger, the failure of food. Amos redefines the deepest famine: not a lack of bread or water, which the body can feel and lament, but a famine of hearing the words of the LORD. When people despise God's word in the day of grace, He may withdraw it, and no hunger is more desperate than a soul that finally seeks God's voice and cannot find it.

dabar.gloss: A word, matter, thing; the davar of the LORD is God's living speech through His prophets. It had come freely and been rejected; now Amos warns of a day when people will stagger from sea to sea seeking the davar and not find it. The word that was scorned when abundant becomes, in judgment, the one thing they crave and cannot obtain, until it comes at last, full and final, in Christ the Word.

basic.context: In the fourth vision God shows Amos a basket of ripe summer fruit and makes a pun on it: the fruit is ripe, and so is the end for Israel. God will spare them no longer. Amos then names the sin behind the sentence: greedy merchants who resent the Sabbath and holy days because they interrupt business, who rig their scales, shrink their measures, and buy the poor for the price of a pair of sandals. The day of judgment will bring earthquake and darkness at noon, and feasts will turn to funerals. Worst of all, God warns of a famine, not of food, but of hearing His word.
basic.hermeneutics: The vision works by wordplay: ripe fruit equals the end. The point is that outward prosperity can mask a nation that is spiritually finished. The sin exposed is religion that keeps the calendar while the heart despises both God and the poor. And the climactic threat teaches a sobering truth: the greatest judgment God can send is to stop speaking, to withdraw the word people would not heed.
basic.originalLanguages: The vision turns on a pun: qayits, summer fruit, sounds like qets, the end. God will send a raab, a famine, not of bread but of hearing the words of the LORD, so that people will seek the dabar, the word, and not find it.
basic.typology: The famine of the word anticipates the long silence before Christ and is answered by the coming of Jesus, the Word made flesh, the final and full speech of God. The ripe fruit that means the end points to the day of judgment that Christ bore, when the sun truly went dark at noon.
basic.christ: Amos warns of a famine of hearing God's word; that hunger is answered in Jesus, the Word who became flesh, God's final speech after the prophets. The darkness at noon that Amos foretells fell literally at the cross, where Christ endured the day of judgment so that for His people the famine of the word ends forever in the living bread of His gospel.
basic.god: God cares fiercely for the poor and remembers every act of exploitation; He swears He will never forget. He is sovereign over creation, able to darken the sun and shake the land. And He reveals that His word is a mercy so precious that its withdrawal is among the most terrible of judgments.
basic.crossReferences: Proverbs 11:1 condemns dishonest scales as an abomination. Deuteronomy 8:3 says man lives by every word from God's mouth. John 1:14 declares the Word became flesh. Matthew 27:45 records the darkness at noon over the crucified Christ.

context.literary: This is the fourth vision, sharing the dialogue form of the third, Amos, what do you see, and delivering another verdict of no longer sparing. Around the vision Amos gathers earlier themes: the trampling of the poor, dishonest trade, the day of the LORD as darkness, and mourning replacing celebration. The famine of the word stands as the rhetorical climax, a judgment worse than the physical disasters that surround it.
context.historical: The New Moon and Sabbath were days when trade paused for worship; the merchants chafed at the interruption. The ephah was a dry measure and the shekel a weight of silver; shrinking the ephah and inflating the shekel meant giving less grain for more money, compounded by rigged scales. Selling the poor for a pair of sandals recalls the opening oracle against Israel in chapter two. Dan and Beersheba marked the northern and southern extremes of the land and were centers of false or corrupted worship.
context.canonical: The pun on the end echoes Ezekiel's later vision that the end has come. The famine of the word illuminates the intertestamental silence between the prophets and the coming of Christ, and it magnifies the gift of the Word made flesh. The darkness at noon becomes one of the most striking links between Amos and the crucifixion.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To announce through the ripe-fruit pun that Israel's time has run out, to expose the greed and contempt for the poor that ripened the judgment, and to warn that the ultimate loss is not bread or water but the very word of God.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The vision and oracles describe the judgment falling on eighth-century Israel. They prescribe, by implication, honest dealing, care for the poor, and reverence for God's word, and they warn every generation against despising the word while it is still freely given.

debates.question: What is the famine of hearing the words of the LORD, and can it happen again?

views.label: The withdrawal of prophetic revelation as judgment
views.case: Because Israel despised God's word when it came freely through the prophets, God threatens to stop sending it; the silence itself is the judgment, and it fits the long prophetic gap before Christ, when no fresh word came.

views.label: A spiritual inability to find God's word though it is present
views.case: People will stagger and seek yet not find, suggesting not merely the absence of prophets but a judicial hardening in which the word, even where available, no longer nourishes or is understood, a danger Scripture warns of wherever the word is persistently scorned.

debates.bearing: Both underline that God's word is a mercy not to be presumed upon. To neglect it while it is near is to risk a day when it is craved and cannot be found; the warning drives us to receive the Word, Christ Himself, while He may be heard.

study.originalLanguages: The fourth vision hinges on a pun. Amos sees a basket of qayits, summer fruit, the ripe late harvest that marks the close of the year, and God answers that the qets, the end, has come upon Israel, the two words sounding almost the same so that the picture of plenty becomes a sentence of finality. The oracle then exposes merchants who shrink the ephah, the grain measure, and inflate the shekel, the silver weight, cheating with rigged scales and buying the poor for a pair of sandals. Against this the LORD swears by the Pride of Jacob never to forget their deeds, and He threatens the gravest judgment of all: a raab, a famine, not of bread but of hearing the words of the LORD, so that people stagger from sea to sea seeking the dabar, the word, and do not find it. The word despised in the day of grace becomes the one thing they cannot obtain.

typology.type: The basket of ripe fruit meaning the end, 8:1-2
typology.fulfillment: The day of judgment that ripened and fell on Christ, who bore the end in Himself so His people might have a new beginning, 2 Corinthians 5:21.

typology.type: The famine of hearing the words of the LORD, 8:11-12
typology.fulfillment: Ended in Jesus, the Word made flesh, God's full and final speech after the prophets, John 1:14, Hebrews 1:1-2.

typology.type: The sun going dark at noon, 8:9
typology.fulfillment: The darkness over the land at the crucifixion, when Christ endured the day of judgment, Matthew 27:45.

study.christ: The most terrible judgment Amos names is a famine of hearing God's word, a hunger of the soul with no relief. That famine is answered once and for all in Jesus, the Word made flesh, the living speech of God who came after the long silence of the prophets and declared, whoever comes to Me will never hunger. The ripe fruit that signaled the end and the sun going dark at noon both find their deepest fulfillment at the cross, where the end of the age fell upon Christ and darkness covered the land from the sixth hour to the ninth. He bore the day of judgment Amos foretold, absorbing its darkness and its finality, so that for all who trust Him the famine of the word is over: the bread of life is given, the word is near, and it will never again be taken away.
study.god: God is revealed as the defender of the poor who keeps an unforgetting record of every fraud and cruelty; He swears He will never forget their deeds. He is the Creator who can turn noon to midnight and make the solid land heave like the Nile, so His judgments carry cosmic weight. Above all He is the God who speaks, and who counts His word so precious that to withdraw it is among the severest sentences He can pass. His silence is a judgment; His speech is a mercy. And the same God who threatens the famine of the word answers it in the end by sending His Son, the Word Himself, proving that His last movement toward a guilty world is not silence but the gift of Christ.

crossReferences.note: Dishonest scales are an abomination to the LORD, the very sin of the merchants who rigged their weights against the poor.
Man does not live by bread alone but by every word from the mouth of God, making a famine of the word the deadliest hunger.
The Word became flesh and dwelt among us, ending the famine of hearing God by God's full self-disclosure in Christ.
Darkness covered the land from noon as Christ was crucified, fulfilling the sign of the sun going down at noon.

study.oneStory: Israel looks ripe with prosperity but is ripe for the end, its greed and contempt for the poor and for God's word having run out His patience, and the worst judgment is a famine of hearing His voice. The story answers that famine in Jesus, the Word made flesh, who bore the darkness at noon and the end of the age at the cross, so that the word once withdrawn is given forever as the bread of life.

quiz.q: What does the basket of ripe summer fruit mean?

options.text: By a pun, the ripe fruit signals that the end has come for Israel.
options.note: Summer fruit, qayits, sounds like end, qets; the harvest picture becomes a sentence of doom.

options.text: That God is promising a good harvest to Israel.
options.note: The fruit is a sign of the end, not a promise of blessing.

options.text: That Israel's time is up before God.
options.note: Right; the wordplay makes the ripe fruit a picture of the arrived end.

options.text: That the people should offer their firstfruits.
options.note: The vision is about judgment ripening, not an offering to bring.

quiz.q: What sin does Amos expose in the greedy merchants?

options.text: They resent the Sabbath, rig their scales, shrink their measures, and buy the poor for a pair of sandals.
options.note: Religion on the calendar while the heart cheats and exploits the poor.

options.text: They forgot to observe the New Moon festival.
options.note: They kept the days outwardly; they simply could not wait for them to end so they could cheat.

options.text: They exploited the poor through dishonest trade.
options.note: Right, and pointedly while still observing the outward forms of worship.

options.text: They charged interest on loans to foreigners.
options.note: The specific sins named are rigged scales and exploiting the poor, not foreign interest.

quiz.q: What is the most severe judgment Amos announces?

options.text: A famine not of bread or water but of hearing the words of the LORD.
options.note: The withdrawal of God's word is the deepest famine of all.

options.text: A literal drought that dries up all the wells.
options.note: Amos explicitly says it is not a thirst for water but for God's word.

options.text: The loss of God's word from among the people.
options.note: Right; the passage frames this as worse than physical hunger.

options.text: An invasion by a foreign army.
options.note: Invasion is implied elsewhere, but the climactic threat here is the famine of the word.

quiz.q: How is the famine of hearing God's word answered in Christ?

options.text: Jesus is the Word made flesh, God's full and final speech, ending the famine for all who come to Him.
options.note: Christ is the living Word who satisfies the deepest hunger.

options.text: It simply means we should read more Scripture.
options.note: A good application, but the passage points ultimately to Christ the Word Himself.

options.text: It shows that God sent more prophets after Amos.
options.note: True that the word continued, but its fulfillment is the Word made flesh.

options.text: It has no relationship to Jesus.
options.note: The famine of the word is answered directly by Christ, the Word of God.

quiz.q: How does the sun going dark at noon point to the cross?

options.text: The darkness Amos foretold fell literally at Calvary, where Christ bore the day of judgment.
options.note: Darkness covered the land at noon as Christ was crucified.

options.text: It only refers to an ordinary solar eclipse in Amos's day.
options.note: It is a sign of judgment that finds its deepest fulfillment at the cross.

options.text: It shows that Jesus also spoke of darkness and judgment.
options.note: True that He did, but the direct link is the noon darkness at His crucifixion.

options.text: It has nothing to do with Christ.
options.note: The Gospels tie the noon darkness directly to the cross.

questions: The merchants kept the Sabbath outwardly but could not wait for it to end so they could cheat. Where does your outward religion mask a heart set on other things?
God swore He would never forget how the poor were exploited. How does knowing that God remembers every injustice shape the way you do business and treat the vulnerable?
Amos warned of a famine of hearing God's word. Are you feeding on God's word now, while it is freely given, or neglecting it?
The word despised in the day of grace became the one thing the people could not find. What would it cost you to treasure Scripture before you are desperate for it?
Jesus is the Word made flesh who ends the famine forever. How does coming to Him as the bread of life satisfy a hunger that nothing else can?

translationNotes.note: The vision turns on a pun impossible to carry fully into English: qayits, summer fruit, sounds like qets, the end. The ripe basket declares that Israel's end has come.

Reduce the ephah and increase the shekel means giving a smaller measure of grain while charging a heavier price in silver; it may also be rendered reduce the measure and increase the price. Combined with dishonest scales, it is deliberate fraud against the poor.

The way of Beersheba may also be rendered the god of Beersheba; the phrase names a corrupted cult at a southern shrine, paired with Dan in the north to show idolatry from one end of the land to the other.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Amos 8:1-14. Poetic line fragments joined into running verse text; footnote markers omitted; em dashes in verses 8, 11, 13, and 14 rendered as commas per house style.
